policies.title: 1. Purpose & 2. Scope
paragraphs: Purpose: Outlines operational standards, professional expectations, and clinical procedures for all staff working within the UHS Ambulance and Hospital Service to ensure safe, effective, and professional care.

Scope: Applies to all UHS personnel, including Student Paramedics, Emergency Care Assistants/Technicians, Paramedics, Senior Paramedics, Critical Care/HEMS, HARTS, Nurses, Doctors, Hospital-based clinical staff, and Supervisors/Command leads.

policies.title: 3. Professional Uniform Standards
paragraphs: All personnel must maintain a clean, professional appearance at all times.

list: Uniform must be clean, well-fitted, and appropriate to the role.
High-visibility jackets must be worn at roadside, outdoor, or hazardous incidents.
Footwear must be black, enclosed, and suitable for emergencies.
ID badges visible at all times.
PPE used appropriately. No unauthorised headgear or non-issued clothing.
Ambulance: Green operational uniform, hi-vis for outdoors, stab vest where issued, clinical PPE.
HARTS: Technical uniform, BA equipment when required, specialist PPE (CBRN, water, confined spaces), helmets, harnesses.
Hospital: Scrubs/tunics, appropriate PPE, hair tied back, no loose jewellery.

paragraphs: Use for intended purpose only. Maintain competency. Report faults immediately. Replenish medical bags after every incident.

list: Defibrillators: Cardiac arrest or clinically indicated arrhythmias only.
Airway & Monitoring: Use according to condition/competency; apply monitoring to unresponsive/unwell patients.
Controlled Drugs: Handled per legislation and policy.

paragraphs: Radios must be used professionally. Blue-lights activated only as required. Check medical equipment at shift start.
Follow all road traffic laws, speed limits, and drive with consideration. Park safely without obstructing access.
Blue-light response ONLY for: Immediately life-threatening emergencies, urgent inter-facility transfers, major incidents, and rapid deployments (HARTS, cardiac arrest, serious trauma).
Principles: Maintain full control, slow down at junctions/red lights, avoid collisions, use appropriate siren tone, and prioritize public/crew safety.
Wear PPE based on risk assessment. Remove yourself from unsafe scenes until supported. Maintain situational awareness.
Assess scenes before approaching. Use safe lifting. Secure patients during transport. Communicate before procedures.
Report hazards/faults. Maintain cleanliness. Do not obstruct exits/equipment.

policies.title: 7. HARTS Operations
paragraphs: HARTS responds to CBRN/HAZMAT, water rescue, confined spaces, major incidents, and structural collapse.
Equipment: BA used only by trained staff. Carry out dynamic risk assessments before entering hazardous zones.
Team Safety: Operate in pairs/teams, maintain radio contact, follow command structure during major incidents.
Treat patients respectfully, remain calm, avoid conflict, and uphold confidentiality. Use clear radio comms and accurate SBAR handovers. Follow national guidelines and participate in training.
Leaders promote safe practice; staff are accountable for actions. Breaches may result in: Verbal advice, written warnings, temporary suspension, removal from duties, or review of professional status.

policies.title: Hospital Lockdown Policy
policies.alert: Notice: A hospital lockdown may only be initiated by UHS Command. No other member of UHS staff has the authority to order this.
paragraphs: Continuity of Care: Triage tents must be established without delay. Treat members of the public within tents. No staff should remain idle; focus on patient care and public safety.
Post in the LOA Request channel. Must include: Start Date, End Date, Reason.
All complaints are confidential. File via the UHS Internal Complaints Form.

clinical.title: Primary Survey (CABCDE)
list: C (Catastrophic Haemorrhage): Direct pressure, field dressing, Celox, Tourniquet, TXA.
A (Airway): Patent/Clear/Occluded. Jaw lift/chin thrust. Use adjuncts.
B (Breathing): Rate and effort. Auscultation. SpO2. Consider oxygen.
C (Circulation): Skin colour/cap refill. Pulse rate/quality. BP.
E (Expose): Undress to check injuries. Cover up ASAP to prevent hypothermia.

paragraphs: Head Tilt-Chin Lift: Standard method.
Jaw Thrust: Use for suspected spinal injuries.
OPA: "Invert, Insert, Rotate, Locate." Use on unconscious patients.
NPA: Insert in nostril, bevel toward septum.
Start if unconscious and not breathing. 30 compressions : 2 breaths. Connect Defib/AED and shock if advised. Repeat for 3 sets. If no ROSC after 10 minutes, reassess.
Valid for chronic illnesses, terminal age. NOT valid for acts of violence (stabbing) or young healthy individuals (Police/Fire).

clinical.title: Common Emergencies
paragraphs: Heart Attack (MI): Chest pain, sweating, left arm pain. Give 300mg Aspirin, GTN (if BP safe), O2.
Anaphylaxis: Swelling, wheeze, low BP. Give IM Adrenaline (0.5mg), high-flow O2.
Asthma Attack: Wheeze, short of breath. Give Salbutamol (Neb/MDI), O2.
Hypoglycaemia: Confusion, sweating, collapse. Give Oral Glucose if awake.
Opioid Overdose: Slow/absent breathing, pinpoint pupils. Give Naloxone, ventilate.
clinical.alert: Transport Immediately If: Chest pain, Major Trauma, Anaphylaxis, Severe Asthma, Stroke, Unconscious, Serious bleeding, Seizure > 5 mins.

clinical.title: Trauma & Burns
paragraphs: Major Bleeding: Direct pressure, bandage, tourniquet.
Head Injury: Keep airway clear, minimise movement, X-ray/MRI needed.
Spinal Precautions: Use if high-energy mechanism, neck/back pain, unconscious. MRI required.
A: Alert | V: Voice | P: Pain | U: Unresponsive
Signs/Symptoms, Allergies, Medications, Past Medical Hx, Last Oral Intake, Events leading up.
Onset, Provoking factors, Quality, Region & Radiation, Severity (0-10), Time.
Balance, Eyes, Face, Arms, Speech, Time.
Age, Time, Mechanism of Injury, Injuries, Signs (Vitals), Treatment administered.

meds.text: Staff may administer medications listed under their current grade, as well as all medications from the grades below them.

rows: Cardiac arrest, major trauma, sepsis, shock, and breathing difficulties

meds.title: Prescription Pad Guide
orderedList: Fill in the patient's full name and CSN number (address not needed).
Select the correct medication based on the Outbreak Treatment list.
Fill out treatment days (Max 4 days currently).
Sign at the bottom and save.
Hand to the patient and direct them to the pharmacy.

driving.title: Emergency Driving Exemptions
paragraphs: Blue-lights should only be used for: Life-threatening emergencies, urgent transfers, major incidents, and rapid deployments.

list: Travelling on the wrong side of the road
Treating RED lights as give way

driving.alert: Speed Rule: Driving at a speed exceeding 20 mph above the speed limit is NOT recommended. Breaching this may result in loss of CERAD certification.

driving.title: Controls & Sirens
paragraphs:
Q: Primary 360 Lights
K: Rear Red emergency lights
Y: Additional lighting
G: Toggle Sirens
R: Cycle Siren Tones
E: Bullhorn

list: Wail: Normal siren, for high-speed roads to warn traffic far ahead.
Yelp: Short range, use when approaching junctions.
Phaser: Very loud, use crossing busy intersections.
Flowing Traffic: Approach in lanes 2/3 with ALL equipment on.
Slow Traffic: Approach on hard shoulder with visual warnings only.
Stationary Traffic: Approach on hard shoulder with ALL equipment on.

paragraphs: Keep a reasonable distance. Utilize DIFFERENT siren tones than the lead vehicle. If transporting to ED, remain behind the box ambulance at all times.
Remain in lane three (closest to central reservation) under blue lights. DO NOT mount pavements to get around vehicles unless entering a property.

mechanics.title: Uniform Configurations
list: Stabvests/Helmets: ONLY to be used during scenes where weapons or dangerous situations are present.
